docs.title: Aprisma
docs.text:
A Python Prisma client backed by SQLAlchemy — no Node.js or Rust at runtime.

Aprisma reads a standard schema.prisma file and generates a fully-typed async Python client. All queries run through SQLAlchemy Core — SQLite, PostgreSQL, and any other SQLAlchemy dialect work out of the box.

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
pip install aprisma

For PostgreSQL:

pip install "aprisma[postgresql]"

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
# Generate the Python client from your schema
aprisma generate --schema schema.prisma

from prisma import Prisma

db = Prisma()
await db.connect("sqlite+aiosqlite:///dev.db")

user = await db.user.create(data={"email": "alice@example.com", "name": "Alice"})

posts = await db.post.find_many(
    where={"author": {"email": "alice@example.com"}, "published": True},
    include={"author": True},
    order={"createdAt": "desc"},
)

await db.disconnect()

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Pure Python runtime — no Node.js process, no Rust binary at query time
  • Async-first — every method is async; built for asyncio
  • Fully typed — Pydantic v2 models, TypedDict query inputs, typed relation attributes
  • Prisma v7 schema — standard schema format; official Prisma CLI handles migrations
  • SQLAlchemy backend — JOIN-based relation filtering, connection pooling, any dialect
  • Rich query API — filtering, ordering, pagination, distinct, include, select, transactions, raw queries, aggregations

docs.title: Why Aprisma?
docs.text: The original prisma-client-py is no longer maintained and requires a Rust query engine and a Node.js subprocess at every query. Aprisma replaces that runtime with a pure SQLAlchemy layer — the Prisma CLI is only needed during development for schema migrations, never at runtime.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Getting Started — build a working app from scratch
  • Prisma CLI Setup — one-time Node.js and migrations setup
  • Schema Reference — field types, relations, attributes
  • API Reference — all query methods

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
Found a bug or have a feature idea? Please open an issue on GitHub Issues.

When reporting a bug, include a minimal reproducible example:

  • The relevant part of your schema.prisma
  • The Python code that triggers the problem
  • The full error message and traceback
  • Which database and driver you are using

When proposing a feature, describe the problem it solves rather than jumping straight to a solution.

Please do not open pull requests. A clear, reproducible issue is more useful than an unsolicited PR and avoids wasted effort on both sides.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide walks through building a small blog application with Aprisma from scratch — from installation to running queries.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+
  • Node.js (for the Prisma CLI — only needed during development). If you haven't set this up yet, follow the Prisma CLI Setup guide first.

docs.title: 1. Install Aprisma
docs.text:
pip install aprisma

For PostgreSQL, add the driver:

pip install "aprisma[postgresql]"

docs.title: 2. Write the schema
docs.text:
Create schema.prisma in your project root:

generator client {
  provider  = "aprisma"
  output    = "./prisma"
  interface = "asyncio"
}

datasource db {
  provider = "sqlite"
}

model User {
  id        Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  email     String   @unique
  name      String
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  posts     Post[]
}

model Post {
  id        Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  title     String
  content   String
  published Boolean  @default(false)
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  authorId  Int
  author    User     @relation(fields: [authorId], references: [id])
}

The generator block tells Aprisma to generate the client into the ./prisma directory. The datasource block sets the database engine — no connection URL is needed here (it is passed at runtime).

docs.title: 3. Push the schema to the database
docs.text:
For local development, db push is the fastest way to apply the schema without creating a migration history:

npx prisma db push --url "file:./dev.db"

For a real project with migrations, use:

npx prisma migrate dev --name init

See the Prisma CLI Setup guide for how migrations work.

docs.title: 4. Generate the Python client
docs.text:
aprisma generate --schema schema.prisma

This creates ./prisma/__init__.py and ./prisma/client.py. The generated Prisma class is what you import in your application.

docs.title: 5. Write the application
docs.text:
Create app.py:

import asyncio
from prisma import Prisma


async def main() -> None:
    db = Prisma()
    await db.connect("sqlite+aiosqlite:///dev.db")

    # --- Create ---
    alice = await db.user.create(
        data={"email": "alice@example.com", "name": "Alice"}
    )
    bob = await db.user.create(
        data={"email": "bob@example.com", "name": "Bob"}
    )

    post1 = await db.post.create(
        data={"title": "Hello from Alice", "content": "First post.", "authorId": alice.id},
        include={"author": True},
    )
    print(f"Created: '{post1.title}' by {post1.author.name}")

    post2 = await db.post.create(
        data={"title": "Hello from Bob", "content": "Bob's post.", "authorId": bob.id},
    )

    # --- Read ---
    # All published posts, newest first
    published = await db.post.find_many(
        where={"published": True},
        order={"createdAt": "desc"},
        include={"author": True},
    )
    print(f"Published: {len(published)} post(s)")

    # Filter through a relation — posts by Alice
    alice_posts = await db.post.find_many(
        where={"author": {"email": "alice@example.com"}},
    )
    print(f"Alice has {len(alice_posts)} post(s)")

    # --- Update ---
    updated = await db.post.update(
        where={"id": post1.id},
        data={"published": True},
    )
    print(f"Published: {updated.title}")

    # --- Count ---
    n = await db.post.count(where={"published": True})
    print(f"{n} published post(s)")

    # --- Upsert ---
    carol = await db.user.upsert(
        where={"email": "carol@example.com"},
        create={"email": "carol@example.com", "name": "Carol"},
        update={"name": "Carol (updated)"},
    )
    print(f"Upserted: {carol.name}")

    # --- Delete ---
    await db.post.delete_many(where={"authorId": bob.id})
    await db.user.delete(where={"id": bob.id})

    await db.disconnect()


asyncio.run(main())

Run it:

python app.py

docs.title: 6. Transactions
docs.text:
Wrap multiple writes in a transaction using the tx() context manager. If any operation raises, all changes are rolled back:

async with db.tx() as tx:
    user = await tx.user.create(data={"email": "dave@example.com", "name": "Dave"})
    await tx.post.create(
        data={"title": "Dave's post", "content": "...", "authorId": user.id}
    )
    # both writes commit together; an exception here rolls back both

docs.title: 7. Raw queries
docs.text:
For queries that go beyond the generated API:

rows = await db.query_raw('SELECT id, email FROM "User" WHERE id = :uid', uid=alice.id)
count = await db.execute_raw('DELETE FROM "Post" WHERE published = ?', False)

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Schema Reference — field types, relations, indexes
  • API Reference — all query methods and parameters
  • Prisma CLI Setup — migrations in production

docs.title: Setting up Prisma CLI in a Python project
docs.text:
Aprisma handles all Python-side database access, but schema migrations are managed by the official Prisma CLI (a Node.js tool). This guide walks through adding the Prisma CLI to a Python project from scratch.

You do not need to know JavaScript or Node.js beyond running the commands below.

docs.title: 1. Install fnm (Fast Node Manager)
docs.text:
fnm manages Node.js versions the same way pyenv manages Python versions.

macOS / LinuxWindows (PowerShell) 
curl -fsSL https://fnm.vercel.app/install | bash

Then follow the printed instructions to add fnm to your shell profile (.bashrc, .zshrc, etc.), or restart your terminal.

winget install Schniz.fnm

Verify:

fnm --version

docs.title: 2. Create a .node-version file
docs.text:
In your project root, create a file that pins the Node.js version:

echo "22" > .node-version

This tells fnm (and CI) which Node version to use. Commit this file.

docs.title: 3. Install Node.js and activate it
docs.text:
fnm install   # installs the version from .node-version
fnm use       # activates it in the current shell
node --version  # should print v22.x.x

To make activation automatic when you cd into the project, add this to your shell profile:

bashzsh 
eval "$(fnm env --use-on-cd)"

eval "$(fnm env --use-on-cd)"

docs.title: 4. Create package.json
docs.text:
Create a minimal package.json in your project root. The private: true flag prevents accidental npm publishing.

npm init -y

Then open package.json and set "private": true:

{
  "name": "my-project-dev",
  "private": true,
  "devDependencies": {}
}

docs.title: 5. Install the Prisma CLI
docs.text:
npm install prisma --save-dev

Commit both package.json and package-lock.json. Colleagues and CI will use npm ci (faster, uses the lockfile exactly) instead of npm install.

Verify:

npx prisma --version

docs.title: 6. Configure Prisma v7 (prisma.config.ts)
docs.text:
Prisma v7 moves the database connection URL out of schema.prisma and into a TypeScript config file.

Create prisma.config.ts in your project root:

import { defineConfig } from "prisma/config";

export default defineConfig({
  schema: "schema.prisma",        // path to your schema.prisma
  migrate: {
    datasourceUrl: process.env.DATABASE_URL ?? "file:./dev.db",
  },
});

Set your DATABASE_URL environment variable (e.g. in a .env file — add .env to .gitignore):

DATABASE_URL="file:./dev.db"

docs.title: 7. Write your schema.prisma
docs.text:
Create schema.prisma (the url property is no longer in the datasource block in v7):

generator client {
  provider = "aprisma"
  output   = "./prisma"
  interface = "asyncio"
}

datasource db {
  provider = "sqlite"
}

model User {
  id    Int    @id @default(autoincrement())
  name  String
  email String @unique
}

docs.title: 8. Run your first migration
docs.text:
npx prisma migrate dev --name init

This creates a prisma/migrations/ directory, generates a SQL migration file, and applies it to your local database. Commit the migration files — they are your schema history.

For subsequent schema changes:

# after editing schema.prisma
npx prisma migrate dev --name describe_your_change

docs.title: 9. Apply migrations in production / CI
docs.text:
npx prisma migrate deploy

This applies all pending migrations without prompting. Use this in deployment pipelines and CI.

docs.title: Common commands
docs.text: Command Description npx prisma migrate dev --name <name> Create and apply a new migration locally npx prisma migrate deploy Apply pending migrations (production / CI) npx prisma migrate status Show which migrations are applied / pending npx prisma db push Sync schema to DB without a migration file (prototyping only) npx prisma studio Open a web UI to browse your data npx prisma format Format schema.prisma

docs.title: .gitignore additions
docs.text:
# Node.js
node_modules/

# Local database files
*.db
*.db-journal

# Environment variables
.env

Commit package.json, package-lock.json, prisma.config.ts, schema.prisma, and everything under prisma/migrations/. Do not commit node_modules/ or .env.

docs.title: Schema Reference
docs.text: Aprisma uses the standard Prisma schema language. This page covers the features Aprisma supports and any Aprisma-specific generator options.

docs.title: Generator block
docs.text:
generator client {
  provider  = "aprisma"
  output    = "./prisma"      # where to write the generated client
  interface = "asyncio"       # only asyncio is supported
}
Option Values Description provider "aprisma" Required — selects the Aprisma generator output path Directory for the generated client.py interface "asyncio" Client interface style

docs.title: Datasource block
docs.text:
datasource db {
  provider = "sqlite"   # or "postgresql"
}

No url is set in the schema — the connection URL is passed to db.connect() at runtime. The provider field is used by the Prisma CLI for migrations.

docs.title: Scalar types
docs.text:
Prisma type Python type Notes String str Int int BigInt int Python int handles arbitrary precision Float float Decimal Decimal decimal.Decimal Boolean bool DateTime datetime datetime.datetime (naive UTC) Bytes bytes Json Any Stored as text; round-trips as Python object

Append ? to make any field nullable (String?, Int?, etc.). A nullable field is None in Python when no value is stored.

docs.title: Default values
docs.text:
id        Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
createdAt DateTime @default(now())
isActive  Boolean  @default(true)
score     Float    @default(0.0)

docs.title: Field attributes
docs.text: Attribute Description @id Marks the primary key @unique Adds a unique constraint; field can be used in find_unique @default(...) Sets a default value @map("col_name") Maps the Prisma field to a different column name @updatedAt Automatically set to the current time on every update

docs.title: Model attributes
docs.text: Attribute Description @@id([fieldA, fieldB]) Composite primary key @@unique([fieldA, fieldB]) Composite unique constraint @@index([fieldA, fieldB]) Creates an index (handled by Prisma CLI) @@map("table_name") Maps the model to a different table name @@ignore Excludes the model from the generated client

docs.title: One-to-many
docs.text:
model User {
  id    Int    @id @default(autoincrement())
  posts Post[]
}

model Post {
  id       Int  @id @default(autoincrement())
  authorId Int
  author   User @relation(fields: [authorId], references: [id])
}

Post.authorId is the foreign key. User.posts is the back-reference list.

docs.title: One-to-one
docs.text:
model User {
  id      Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  profile Profile?
}

model Profile {
  id     Int  @id @default(autoincrement())
  userId Int  @unique
  user   User @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
}

docs.title: Many-to-many (explicit join table)
docs.text:
model Post {
  id   Int       @id @default(autoincrement())
  tags PostTag[]
}

model Tag {
  id    Int       @id @default(autoincrement())
  name  String    @unique
  posts PostTag[]
}

model PostTag {
  postId Int  @map("post_id")
  tagId  Int  @map("tag_id")
  post   Post @relation(fields: [postId], references: [id])
  tag    Tag  @relation(fields: [tagId], references: [id])

  @@id([postId, tagId])
}

docs.title: Enums
docs.text:
enum Role {
  USER
  ADMIN
  MODERATOR
}

model User {
  id   Int    @id @default(autoincrement())
  role Role   @default(USER)
}

Enums are stored as strings. The generated Python client exposes them as str values matching the Prisma enum member names.

docs.title: Unsupported types
docs.text: Fields declared with Unsupported("...") are excluded from the generated client. Use raw queries (query_raw, execute_raw) to interact with those columns.

docs.title: count
docs.text:
Returns the number of records matching the filter.

n = await db.post.count()                           # total rows
n = await db.post.count(where={"published": True})  # filtered

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description where dict Filter conditions take int Count only within a window of this size skip int Skip this many rows before the window select dict[str, bool] Count non-null values per field (returns dict)

docs.title: Field-level counts
docs.text:
counts = await db.post.count(select={"title": True, "publishedAt": True})
# {"title": 42, "publishedAt": 17}
# publishedAt is lower because nullable rows are not counted

docs.title: group_by
docs.text:
Groups records by one or more fields and computes aggregations.

results = await db.post.group_by(
    by=["authorId"],
    count={"_all": True},
    avg={"score": True},
    order_by={"_avg": {"score": "desc"}},
)
for row in results:
    print(row["authorId"], row["_count"]["_all"], row["_avg"]["score"])

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description by list[str] Fields to group by where dict Filter applied before grouping count dict or True Count records per group avg dict[str, bool] Average of numeric fields sum_ dict[str, bool] Sum of numeric fields min_ dict[str, bool] Minimum value max_ dict[str, bool] Maximum value order_by dict or list[dict] Sort the groups take int Limit number of groups returned skip int Skip this many groups

docs.title: Return shape
docs.text:
Each item in the result list contains the group-by field values plus nested aggregation dicts:

{
    "authorId": 1,
    "_count": {"_all": 5},
    "_avg": {"score": 3.8},
    "_sum": {"score": 19.0},
}

docs.title: create
docs.text:
Creates a single record and returns it.

user = await db.user.create(
    data={"email": "alice@example.com", "name": "Alice"}
)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description data dict Field values for the new record include dict Relations to load on the returned record

docs.title: Nullable fields
docs.text:
Omitting a nullable field stores NULL. You can also pass None explicitly:

post = await db.post.create(
    data={"title": "Draft", "content": "...", "authorId": 1, "publishedAt": None}
)

docs.title: Nested writes — connect
docs.text:
Link to an existing related record by its unique field:

post = await db.post.create(
    data={
        "title": "My post",
        "content": "...",
        "author": {"connect": {"email": "alice@example.com"}},
    },
    include={"author": True},
)

docs.title: Nested writes — connectOrCreate
docs.text:
Connect to an existing record or create it if it does not exist:

post = await db.post.create(
    data={
        "title": "My post",
        "content": "...",
        "author": {
            "connectOrCreate": {
                "where": {"email": "alice@example.com"},
                "create": {"email": "alice@example.com", "name": "Alice"},
            }
        },
    }
)

docs.title: Errors
docs.text:
• Unique constraint violation → UniqueViolationError
  • Foreign key constraint violation → ForeignKeyViolationError

docs.title: create_many
docs.text:
Creates multiple records in a single round-trip. Returns the number of rows inserted.

count = await db.post.create_many(
    data=[
        {"title": "Post 1", "content": "...", "authorId": 1},
        {"title": "Post 2", "content": "...", "authorId": 1},
    ]
)
print(count)  # 2

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
Parameter Type Description data list[dict] List of records to insert skip_duplicates bool Skip rows that violate a unique constraint instead of raising

create_many does not support include — use find_many afterwards if you need the created records with relations.

docs.title: delete
docs.text:
Deletes a single record and returns it. Returns None if no record matches where.

deleted = await db.post.delete(where={"id": 1})
if deleted:
    print(f"Deleted: {deleted.title}")

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
Parameter Type Description where dict Unique field(s) to identify the record

where must reference a field marked @id or @unique in the schema.

docs.title: delete_many
docs.text:
Deletes all records matching where. Returns the number of rows deleted.

count = await db.post.delete_many(where={"published": False})
print(f"Deleted {count} draft(s)")

Delete everything in a table:

count = await db.post.delete_many()

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
Parameter Type Description where dict Filter conditions (same syntax as find_many). Omit to delete all rows.

where supports the same filter and relation-filter syntax as find_many. Returns 0 if no records match.

docs.title: find_many
docs.text:
Returns a list of records matching the given criteria.

records = await db.post.find_many(
    where={"published": True},
    include={"author": True},
    order={"createdAt": "desc"},
    take=10,
    skip=0,
)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description where dict Filter conditions include dict Relations to load order dict or list[dict] Sort order take int Maximum number of records to return skip int Number of records to skip (offset pagination) distinct list[str] Deduplicate by these fields cursor dict Cursor for keyset pagination select dict[str, bool] Return only selected fields

docs.title: where
docs.text:
Scalar fields match by equality or by filter operators:

# Equality
where={"published": True}

# Filter operators
where={"title": {"contains": "hello"}}
where={"score": {"gte": 4.5}}
where={"id": {"in_": [1, 2, 3]}}

Supported operators: equals, not_, in_, not_in, lt, lte, gt, gte, contains, startswith, endswith

Null checks: 

where={"bio": None}              # IS NULL
where={"bio": {"not_": None}}   # IS NOT NULL

Compound operators: 

where={"AND": [{"published": True}, {"score": {"gte": 4.0}}]}
where={"OR": [{"title": {"contains": "hello"}}, {"title": {"contains": "world"}}]}
where={"NOT": {"published": True}}

Relation filters — filter through a related model's fields:

# Posts whose author has a specific email (uses a JOIN internally)
where={"author": {"email": "alice@example.com"}}

# Users who have at least one published post (correlated EXISTS)
where={"posts": {"some": {"published": True}}}

# Users with no published posts
where={"posts": {"none": {"published": True}}}

docs.title: include
docs.text:
Load related records alongside each result. The value is True to load all fields, or a dict for nested options:

# Simple include
include={"author": True}

# Nested include
include={"author": {"include": {"profile": True}}}

# Include with sub-filter
include={"posts": {"where": {"published": True}, "order": {"createdAt": "desc"}, "take": 5}}

docs.title: order
docs.text:
Sort by one or more fields. Each entry is a {field: "asc" | "desc"} dict:

order={"createdAt": "desc"}
order=[{"score": "desc"}, {"title": "asc"}]

docs.title: take / skip
docs.text:
Offset pagination:

# Page 2 of 10
records = await db.post.find_many(take=10, skip=10)

docs.title: distinct
docs.text:
Return one record per unique combination of the given fields:

# One post per author
posts = await db.post.find_many(distinct=["authorId"], order={"createdAt": "desc"})

docs.title: cursor
docs.text:
Keyset pagination — more efficient than large skip offsets. Pass the unique field(s) of the last record from the previous page:

# First page
page1 = await db.post.find_many(take=10, order={"id": "asc"})

# Next page
page2 = await db.post.find_many(
    take=10,
    cursor={"id": page1[-1].id},
    order={"id": "asc"},
)

docs.title: select
docs.text:
Return only specific scalar fields. Omitted fields are None on the returned model:

posts = await db.post.find_many(select={"id": True, "title": True})
# post.content is None

select and include can be combined — selected scalar fields plus loaded relations.

docs.title: find_unique
docs.text:
Returns a single record by a unique field (@id or @unique). Returns None if no match is found.

user = await db.user.find_unique(where={"id": 1})
user = await db.user.find_unique(where={"email": "alice@example.com"})

# With relations
user = await db.user.find_unique(
    where={"id": 1},
    include={"posts": True, "profile": True},
)

where must contain at least one field marked @id or @unique in the schema. Passing a non-unique field raises ValueError.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description where dict Unique field(s) to match include dict Relations to load select dict[str, bool] Return only selected fields

docs.title: find_unique_or_raise
docs.text:
Same as find_unique but raises RecordNotFoundError instead of returning None:

from aprisma.errors import RecordNotFoundError

try:
    user = await db.user.find_unique_or_raise(where={"id": 999})
except RecordNotFoundError:
    print("not found")

docs.title: find_first
docs.text:
Returns the first record matching the filter, or None. Unlike find_unique, there is no uniqueness requirement on the where fields:

# First published post, newest first
post = await db.post.find_first(
    where={"published": True},
    order={"createdAt": "desc"},
)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description where dict Filter conditions (same syntax as find_many) include dict Relations to load order dict or list[dict] Sort order skip int Skip this many records before taking the first select dict[str, bool] Return only selected fields

docs.title: find_first_or_raise
docs.text: Same as find_first but raises RecordNotFoundError instead of returning None.

docs.title: Transactions
docs.text:
Use the tx() async context manager to run multiple operations atomically. If any operation raises an exception, all changes are rolled back.

async with db.tx() as tx:
    user = await tx.user.create(data={"email": "alice@example.com", "name": "Alice"})
    await tx.post.create(
        data={"title": "First post", "content": "...", "authorId": user.id}
    )
    # both writes commit when the block exits without error

Every model delegate is available on tx with the same API as on db. An exception inside the block (including a constraint violation) automatically rolls back the transaction.

docs.title: Raw queries
docs.text: For queries that go beyond the generated API — complex JOINs, database-specific functions, bulk operations — use the raw query methods on the client.

docs.title: query_raw
docs.text:
Executes a SELECT and returns a list of dict rows.

rows = await db.query_raw(
    'SELECT id, email FROM "User" WHERE id = :uid',
    uid=1,
)
# [{"id": 1, "email": "alice@example.com"}]

Positional parameters — use ? placeholders and pass values as positional arguments:

rows = await db.query_raw('SELECT * FROM "Post" WHERE id = ?', 42)

Named parameters — use :name placeholders and pass values as keyword arguments:

rows = await db.query_raw(
    'SELECT * FROM "Post" WHERE author_id = :aid AND published = :pub',
    aid=1, pub=True,
)

docs.title: query_first
docs.text:
Like query_raw but returns only the first row as a dict, or None if there are no results.

row = await db.query_first('SELECT * FROM "User" WHERE email = :e', e="alice@example.com")
if row:
    print(row["id"])

docs.title: execute_raw
docs.text:
Executes a non-SELECT statement (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DDL) and returns the number of rows affected.

count = await db.execute_raw(
    'UPDATE "Post" SET published = ? WHERE author_id = ?',
    True, 1,
)
print(f"{count} row(s) updated")

docs.title: update
docs.text:
Updates a single record and returns it. Raises RecordNotFoundError if no record matches where.

post = await db.post.update(
    where={"id": 1},
    data={"published": True, "title": "Updated title"},
)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description where dict Unique field(s) to identify the record data dict Fields to update include dict Relations to load on the returned record

docs.title: Setting a field to null
docs.text:
Pass None to clear a nullable field:

await db.post.update(where={"id": 1}, data={"publishedAt": None})

docs.title: Atomic numeric updates
docs.text:
Update numeric fields relative to their current value, without a read-modify-write cycle:

# Increment view count by 1
await db.post.update(
    where={"id": 1},
    data={"viewCount": {"increment": 1}},
)

# Multiply score by 1.1
await db.post.update(
    where={"id": 1},
    data={"score": {"multiply": 1.1}},
)

Supported operators: increment, decrement, multiply, divide

docs.title: Nested writes — connect / disconnect
docs.text:
Reassign or clear a FK-side relation:

# Reassign post to a different author
await db.post.update(
    where={"id": 1},
    data={"author": {"connect": {"email": "bob@example.com"}}},
)

# Clear a nullable relation
await db.post.update(
    where={"id": 1},
    data={"category": {"disconnect": True}},
)

docs.title: update_many
docs.text:
Updates all records matching where. Returns the number of rows affected.

count = await db.post.update_many(
    where={"published": False},
    data={"published": True},
)
print(count)  # number of rows updated

where supports the same filter syntax as find_many. Returns 0 if no records match.

docs.title: upsert
docs.text:
Creates a record if it does not exist, or updates it if it does. Returns the record.

user = await db.user.upsert(
    where={"email": "alice@example.com"},
    create={"email": "alice@example.com", "name": "Alice"},
    update={"name": "Alice (updated)"},
)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description where dict Unique field(s) to look up the existing record create dict Data for the new record if it does not exist update dict Data to apply if the record already exists include dict Relations to load on the returned record
